mnemonics.title: Gradient Rules Made Easy
mnemonics.content: TRACEr: For trace(A^T X), gradient is A; for tr(AX), gradient is A^T; for tr(X), gradient is I; for tr(X^-1), gradient is -(X^-1)^T X^-1

mnemonics.title: Jacobian vs Hessian
mnemonics.content: JACK the HACKER: Jacobian is First-order (J from 'Just'), Hessian is Second-order (H from 'Higher'). J maps vectors to vectors, H maps vectors to scalars' curvature.

mnemonics.title: Definiteness Signs
mnemonics.content: POSitive DEF: Positive Definite → all eigenvalues > 0; NEGative DEF → all eigenvalues < 0; INDEF → mixed signs; SEMI → ≥ 0 or ≤ 0.

acronyms.expansion: Higher-Order Elementary Second Derivative matrix, Symmetric, Includes AllNecessary curvature information
acronyms.usage: The Hessian matrix captures second-order information about the function's curvature, essential for determining the nature of critical points.

acronyms.expansion: Just A Collection Of Basic Unknown Integrals - Just A Convenient Ordered Bundle
acronyms.usage: Used for vector-valued functions to represent all first-order partial derivatives in matrix form.

acronyms.expansion: FRequent Operations Between Elements - Usually Norm In Matrices
acronyms.usage: The Frobenius norm is the Euclidean norm for matrices, computed as sqrt of sum of squared elements.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an ant on a hill - gradient points uphill (steepest ascent), negative gradient points downhill (steepest descent). For 2D, think contour lines - gradient is perpendicular to contours pointing to higher values.

Think of the Hessian as describing the shape of a bowl: positive definite = upward opening bowl (cup); negative definite = downward opening bowl (cap); indefinite = saddle shape - goes up in some directions, down in others.
For ∇_X f where X is a matrix, visualize a grid where each cell contains the partial derivative ∂f/∂x_ij. The gradient is another matrix of the same size showing the sensitivity at each position.

analogies.analogy: Like a blindfolded hiker feeling the slope with their feet - they step in the direction that slopes downward most steeply to find the valley (minimum).
If gradient is speed (first derivative), Jacobian is all directions of motion, and Hessian is like an accelerometer showing how quickly you're accelerating in each direction.
Like a basketball - push from any direction and it pushes back. A negative definite matrix is like an upside-down bowl - things roll away from the center.

Think of a landscape with hills and valleys: peaks (local max), valleys (local min), and mountain passes (saddle points). The gradient tells you which way to go, zero gradient means you're on flat ground or peak/valley.
